commit da96bc487dd65ebb6931e5c10f38651c6851b3dd
Author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document the OOM killer

This commit documents the OOM killer in mm/oom.rst. It covers the scoring
heuristic, allocation constraints, OOM reaper, process_mrelease syscall,
and sysctl knobs.

> diff --git a/Documentation/mm/oom.rst b/Documentation/mm/oom.rst
> index 18e9e40c1ec1..2259f871a4a7 100644
> --- a/Documentation/mm/oom.rst
> +++ b/Documentation/mm/oom.rst

[ ... ]

> +The ``totalpages`` baseline depends on the allocation constraint:
> +
> +- **Unconstrained**: all RAM plus swap.
> +- **Cpuset**: memory on nodes in the current cpuset.
> +- **Memory policy**: memory on nodes in the current mempolicy.

Should swap space also be included for the Cpuset and Memory policy
constraints?

Looking at constrained_alloc() in mm/oom_kill.c, oc->totalpages is
initialized with total_swap_pages before adding the node-specific RAM for
both of these constraints.

> +- **Memory cgroup**: the cgroup's memory limit.

[ ... ]

> +The reaper gives the victim a short window to exit naturally before
> +intervening.  It walks the victim's VMAs in reverse and calls
> +``unmap_page_range()`` to release physical pages.  Once reaping completes

This isn't a bug, but does the OOM reaper actually call unmap_page_range()?

In mm/oom_kill.c, the OOM reaper uses mas_for_each_rev() and calls
zap_vma_for_reaping(), which internally uses __zap_vma_range(). The
unmap_page_range() function does not exist in this context.

> +(or is no longer possible), the mm is marked ``MMF_OOM_SKIP`` so the OOM

[ ... ]

> +``vm.oom_kill_allocating_task``
> +  When non-zero, kill the task that triggered the OOM rather than scanning
> +  for the largest process.

Does this sysctl apply to memory cgroup (memcg) OOM events?

In out_of_memory() within mm/oom_kill.c, the code explicitly requires
!is_memcg_oom(oc) before evaluating this sysctl, meaning it is ignored
during memcg OOMs.

> +
> +``vm.oom_dump_tasks``

Author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

    Docs/mm: document the OOM killer

    ("mm,doc: Add new documentation structure") as part of the structured
    memory management documentation following Mel Gorman's book outline.
    
    Cover the scoring heuristic, allocation constraints, OOM reaper,
    process_mrelease syscall, and sysctl knobs.
    
    Signed-off-by: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

diff --git a/Documentation/mm/oom.rst b/Documentation/mm/oom.rst
index 18e9e40c1ec1..2259f871a4a7 100644
--- a/Documentation/mm/oom.rst
+++ b/Documentation/mm/oom.rst
@@ -3,3 +3,70 @@
 ======================
 Out Of Memory Handling
 ======================
+
+When the kernel cannot satisfy a memory allocation after exhausting reclaim,
+compaction, and memory reserves, it invokes the OOM killer to terminate a
+process and free memory.  The implementation is in ``mm/oom_kill.c``.
+
+Victim Selection
+================
+
+The OOM killer scores every eligible process and kills the one with the
+highest score.  The score is the sum of the process's resident pages, swap
+entries, and page table pages.  This sum is then adjusted by the per-process
+``oom_score_adj`` tunable (range -1000 to 1000, default 0), which biases
+the score by ``oom_score_adj * totalpages / 1000``.  Setting
+``oom_score_adj`` to -1000 disables OOM killing for that process entirely.
+
+The ``totalpages`` baseline depends on the allocation constraint:
+
+- **Unconstrained**: all RAM plus swap.
+- **Cpuset**: memory on nodes in the current cpuset.
+- **Memory policy**: memory on nodes in the current mempolicy.
+- **Memory cgroup**: the cgroup's memory limit.
+
+Only processes that can use memory within the constraint are considered.
+Kernel threads and init are never eligible.
+
+OOM Reaper
+==========
+
+Sending SIGKILL does not immediately free memory — the victim must be
+scheduled, unwind its stack, and tear down its address space.  To speed
+this up, the OOM reaper kernel thread (available on MMU systems) proactively
+unmaps the victim's anonymous and private pages without waiting for the
+victim to exit.
+
+The reaper gives the victim a short window to exit naturally before
+intervening.  It walks the victim's VMAs in reverse and calls
+``unmap_page_range()`` to release physical pages.  Once reaping completes
+(or is no longer possible), the mm is marked ``MMF_OOM_SKIP`` so the OOM
+killer skips it in future invocations.
+
+Before reaping, the mm is marked ``MMF_UNSTABLE`` to signal page fault
+handlers that private mappings may have been zeroed and are no longer
+reliable.
+
+process_mrelease
+================
+
+The ``process_mrelease(pidfd, flags)`` system call lets userspace OOM
+managers (such as systemd-oomd or Android's lmkd) trigger the same reaping
+mechanism on a dying process without waiting for the kernel OOM killer.
+It operates on a process that is already exiting and performs the same
+address space teardown that the OOM reaper would.
+
+Sysctl Knobs
+============
+
+``vm.panic_on_oom``
+  0 (default): kill a process.  1: panic on unconstrained OOM only.
+  2: always panic.
+
+``vm.oom_kill_allocating_task``
+  When non-zero, kill the task that triggered the OOM rather than scanning
+  for the largest process.
+
+``vm.oom_dump_tasks``
+  When non-zero (default), dump a table of all eligible tasks and their
+  memory usage to the kernel log before killing.

No barriers really needed because unmapping\n\t * should imply barriers already and the reader would hit a page fault\n\t * if it stumbled over a reaped memory.\n\t */\n\tmm_flags_set(MMF_UNSTABLE, mm);\n\n\t/*\n\t * It might start racing with the dying task and compete for shared\n\t * resources - e.g. page table lock contention has been observed.\n\t * Reduce those races by reaping the oom victim from the other end\n\t * of the address space.\n\t */\n\tmas_for_each_rev(&mas, vma, 0) {\n\t\tif (vma->vm_flags & (VM_HUGETLB|VM_PFNMAP))\n\t\t\tcontinue;\n\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * Only anonymous pages have a good chance to be dropped\n\t\t * without additional steps which we cannot afford as we\n\t\t * are OOM already.\n\t\t *\n\t\t * We do not even care about fs backed pages because all\n\t\t * which are reclaimable have already been reclaimed and\n\t\t * we do not want to block exit_mmap by keeping mm ref\n\t\t * count elevated without a good reason.\n\t\t */\n\t\tif (vma_is_anonymous(vma) || !(vma->vm_flags & VM_SHARED)) {\n\t\t\tif (zap_vma_for_reaping(vma))\n\t\t\t\tret = false;\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\treturn ret;\n}\n\n/*\n * Reaps the address space of the given task.\n *\n * Returns true on success and false if none or part of the address space\n * has been reclaimed and the caller should retry later.\n */\nstatic bool oom_reap_task_mm(struct task_struct *tsk, struct mm_struct *mm)\n{\n\tbool ret = true;\n\n\tif (!mmap_read_trylock(mm)) {\n\t\ttrace_skip_task_reaping(tsk->pid);\n\t\treturn false;\n\t}\n\n\t/*\n\t * MMF_OOM_SKIP is set by exit_mmap when the OOM reaper can't\n\t * work on the mm anymore. The check for MMF_OOM_SKIP must run\n\t * under mmap_lock for reading because it serializes against the\n\t * mmap_write_lock();mmap_write_unlock() cycle in exit_mmap().\n\t */\n\tif (mm_flags_test(MMF_OOM_SKIP, mm)) {\n\t\ttrace_skip_task_reaping(tsk->pid);\n\t\tgoto out_unlock;\n\t}\n\n\ttrace_start_task_reaping(tsk->pid);\n\n\t/* failed to reap part of the address space. MM_SHMEMPAGES)));\nout_finish:\n\ttrace_finish_task_reaping(tsk->pid);\nout_unlock:\n\tmmap_read_unlock(mm);\n\n\treturn ret;\n}\n\n#define MAX_OOM_REAP_RETRIES 10\nstatic void oom_reap_task(struct The freezer will thaw the tasks that are OOM\n\t * victims regardless of the PM freezing and cgroup freezing states.\n\t */\n\tthaw_process(tsk);\n\tatomic_inc(&oom_victims);\n\tcred = get_task_cred(tsk);\n\ttrace_mark_victim(tsk, cred->uid.val);\n\tput_cred(cred);\n}\n\n/**\n * exit_oom_victim - note the exit of an OOM victim\n */\nvoid exit_oom_victim(void)\n{\n\tclear_thread_flag(TIF_MEMDIE);\n\n\tif (!atomic_dec_return(&oom_victims))\n\t\twake_up_all(&oom_victims_wait);\n}\n\n/**\n * oom_killer_enable - enable OOM killer\n */\nvoid oom_killer_enable(void)\n{\n\toom_killer_disabled = false;\n\tpr_info(\"OOM killer enabled.\\n\");\n}\n\n/**\n * oom_killer_disable - disable OOM killer\n * @timeout: maximum timeout to wait for oom victims in jiffies\n *\n * Forces all page allocations to fail rather than trigger OOM killer.\n * Will block and wait until all OOM victims are killed or the given\n * timeout expires.\n *\n * The function cannot be called when there are runnable user tasks because\n * the userspace would see unexpected allocation failures as a result. Any\n * new usage of this function should be consulted with MM people.\n *\n * Returns true if successful and false if the OOM killer cannot be\n * disabled.\n */\nbool oom_killer_disable(signed long timeout)\n{\n\tsigned long ret;\n\n\t/*\n\t * Make sure to not race with an ongoing OOM killer. If set to the minimum value (`OOM_SCORE_ADJ_MIN`), it effectively disables OOM killing for the process, which is handled correctly in the code as a `LONG_MIN` return. So far, so good.

Now, let's consider how the `totalpages` baseline is determined, which is crucial for normalization.     "content": "{"content":"commit da96bc487dd65ebb6931e5c10f38651c6851b3dd\nAuthor: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>\nDate:   Sat Mar 14 15:25:18 2026 +0000\n\n    Docs/mm: document the OOM killer\n    \n    Fill in the oom.rst stub that was created in commit 481cc97349d6\n    (\"mm,doc: Add new documentation structure\") as part of the structured\n    memory management documentation following Mel Gorman's book outline.\n    \n    Cover the scoring heuristic, allocation constraints, OOM reaper,\n    process_mrelease syscall, and sysctl knobs.\n    \n    Signed-off-by: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>\n\ndiff --git a/Documentation/mm/oom.rst b/Documentation/mm/oom.rst\nindex 18e9e40c1ec1..2259f871a4a7 100644\n--- a/Documentation/mm/oom.rst\n+++ b/Documentation/mm/oom.rst\n@@ -3,3 +3,70 @@\n ======================\n Out Of Memory Handling\n ======================\n+\n+When the kernel cannot satisfy a memory allocation after exhausting reclaim,\n+compaction, and memory reserves, it invokes the OOM killer to terminate a\n+process and free memory.  The implementation is in ``mm/oom_kill.c``.\n+\n+Victim Selection\n+================\n+\n+The OOM killer scores every eligible process and kills the one with the\n+highest score.  The score is the sum of the process's resident pages, swap\n+entries, and page table pages.  This sum is then adjusted by the per-process\n+``oom_score_adj`` tunable (range -1000 to 1000, default 0), which biases\n+the score by ``oom_score_adj * totalpages / 1000``.  Setting\n+``oom_score_adj`` to -1000 disables OOM killing for that process entirely.\n+\n+The ``totalpages`` baseline depends on the allocation constraint:\n+\n+- **Unconstrained**: all RAM plus swap.\n+- **Cpuset**: memory on nodes in the current cpuset.\n+- **Memory policy**: memory on nodes in the current mempolicy.\n+- **Memory cgroup**: the cgroup's memory limit.\n+\n+Only processes that can use memory within the constraint are considered.\n+Kernel threads and init are never eligible.\n+\n+OOM Reaper\n+==========\n+\n+Sending SIGKILL does not immediately free memory — the victim must be\n+scheduled, unwind its stack, and tear down its address space.  To speed\n+this up, the OOM reaper kernel thread (available on MMU systems) proactively\n+unmaps the victim's anonymous and private pages without waiting for the\n+victim to exit.\n+\n+The reaper gives the victim a short window to exit naturally before\n+intervening.  It walks the victim's VMAs in reverse and calls\n+``unmap_page_range()`` to release physical pages.  Once reaping completes\n+(or is no longer possible), the mm is marked ``MMF_OOM_SKIP`` so the OOM\n+killer skips it in future invocations.\n+\n+Before reaping, the mm is marked ``MMF_UNSTABLE`` to signal page fault\n+handlers that private mappings may have been zeroed and are no longer\n+reliable.\n+\n+process_mrelease\n+================\n+\n+The ``process_mrelease(pidfd, flags)`` system call lets userspace OOM\n+managers (such as systemd-oomd or Android's lmkd) trigger the same reaping\n+mechanism on a dying process without waiting for the kernel OOM killer.\n+It operates on a process that is already exiting and performs the same\n+address space teardown that the OOM reaper would.\n+\n+Sysctl Knobs\n+============\n+\n+``vm.panic_on_oom``\n+  0 (default): kill a process.  1: panic on unconstrained OOM only.\n+  2: always panic.\n+\n+``vm.oom_kill_allocating_task``\n+  When non-zero, kill the task that triggered the OOM rather than scanning\n+  for the largest process.\n+\n+``vm.oom_dump_tasks``\n+  When non-zero (default), dump a table of all eligible tasks and their\n+  memory usage to the kernel log before killing.\n"}",

    "content": "{"content":"diff --git a/Documentation/mm/oom.rst b/Documentation/mm/oom.rst\nindex 18e9e40c1ec1..2259f871a4a7 100644\n--- a/Documentation/mm/oom.rst\n+++ b/Documentation/mm/oom.rst\n@@ -3,3 +3,70 @@\n ======================\n Out Of Memory Handling\n ======================\n+\n+When the kernel cannot satisfy a memory allocation after exhausting reclaim,\n+compaction, and memory reserves, it invokes the OOM killer to terminate a\n+process and free memory.  The implementation is in ``mm/oom_kill.c``.\n+\n+Victim Selection\n+================\n+\n+The OOM killer scores every eligible process and kills the one with the\n+highest score.  The score is the sum of the process's resident pages, swap\n+entries, and page table pages.  This sum is then adjusted by the per-process\n+``oom_score_adj`` tunable (range -1000 to 1000, default 0), which biases\n+the score by ``oom_score_adj * totalpages / 1000``.  Setting\n+``oom_score_adj`` to -1000 disables OOM killing for that process entirely.\n+\n+The ``totalpages`` baseline depends on the allocation constraint:\n+\n+- **Unconstrained**: all RAM plus swap.\n+- **Cpuset**: memory on nodes in the current cpuset.\n+- **Memory policy**: memory on nodes in the current mempolicy.\n+- **Memory cgroup**: the cgroup's memory limit.\n+\n+Only processes that can use memory within the constraint are considered.\n+Kernel threads and init are never eligible.\n+\n+OOM Reaper\n+==========\n+\n+Sending SIGKILL does not immediately free memory — the victim must be\n+scheduled, unwind its stack, and tear down its address space.  To speed\n+this up, the OOM reaper kernel thread (available on MMU systems) proactively\n+unmaps the victim's anonymous and private pages without waiting for the\n+victim to exit.\n+\n+The reaper gives the victim a short window to exit naturally before\n+intervening.  It walks the victim's VMAs in reverse and calls\n+``unmap_page_range()`` to release physical pages.  Once reaping completes\n+(or is no longer possible), the mm is marked ``MMF_OOM_SKIP`` so the OOM\n+killer skips it in future invocations.\n+\n+Before reaping, the mm is marked ``MMF_UNSTABLE`` to signal page fault\n+handlers that private mappings may have been zeroed and are no longer\n+reliable.\n+\n+process_mrelease\n+================\n+\n+The ``process_mrelease(pidfd, flags)`` system call lets userspace OOM\n+managers (such as systemd-oomd or Android's lmkd) trigger the same reaping\n+mechanism on a dying process without waiting for the kernel OOM killer.\n+It operates on a process that is already exiting and performs the same\n+address space teardown that the OOM reaper would.\n+\n+Sysctl Knobs\n+============\n+\n+``vm.panic_on_oom``\n+  0 (default): kill a process.  1: panic on unconstrained OOM only.\n+  2: always panic.\n+\n+``vm.oom_kill_allocating_task``\n+  When non-zero, kill the task that triggered the OOM rather than scanning\n+  for the largest process.\n+\n+``vm.oom_dump_tasks``\n+  When non-zero (default), dump a table of all eligible tasks and their\n+  memory usage to the kernel log before killing.\n"}",

	adj *= totalpages / 1000;
	points += adj;

Okay, let's unpack these thoughts on the OOM killer. The document states that "Kernel threads and init are never eligible."  My first thought is, is that entirely accurate?  I immediately honed in on `init`. `is_global_init(p)` is the key, and that specifically checks if the process ID is 1 in the *initial* PID namespace.  So, the global init is indeed immune. However, and this is crucial, `init` processes *within* PID namespaces are killable!

But wait, this opens another question. What if a PID namespace itself OOMs or hits a memcg OOM? Then, that namespace's `init` *is* killable. The documentation seems to be misleading. While namespace `init` threads might ignore a `SIGKILL` if they don't have a signal handler, the OOM killer explicitly forces a `SIGKILL` by setting `TIF_MEMDIE`. Remember, if you kill a namespace `init`, the entire namespace dies. So, `is_global_init` correctly identifies that only *global* init is truly immune. The statement in the document is technically correct *if* it specifically means the *global* init.

Now, let's switch gears and consider the `oom_score_adj`. The doc says the score is the sum of resident pages, swap entries, and page table pages. Then this sum is biased by `oom_score_adj * totalpages / 1000`. But wait. I need to be more precise here. I initially thought it *biased* the score, meaning the final score would be the sum divided by a factor. But that's not right. What the statement means is that `oom_score_adj` *adds*  `oom_score_adj * totalpages / 1000` to the raw score. So, a task with an `oom_score_adj` of 1000 effectively adds `totalpages` (the total amount of RAM) to its score.  Yes, the maximum score is not simply doubled; `points` is just the sum of RSS, swap and page tables. It makes sense, as the maximum score is generally bounded by `totalpages`. Okay, the document is correct in describing this part.

Finally, the `OOM Reaper`. The documentation claims the reaper "gives the victim a short window to exit naturally before intervening." I wanted to verify this with code. I looked at `oom_reap_task` and see that it loops, trying a `down_read_trylock` on `mmap_lock` a few times, waiting a short amount of time (`HZ/10`).  The Reaper *doesn't* wait unless it fails to take the lock. The OOM killer wakes it with `wake_oom_reaper`. So I needed to verify how this works. The `wake_oom_reaper` function queues the victim onto `oom_reaper_list` and then wakes up the reaper thread to process the victim immediately. The small wait inside of `oom_reap_task` happens when the reaper is unable to acquire the mmap lock.

    "thought": "**Reviewing the OOM Killer Documentation**

Okay, let's go through this OOM killer documentation again with a fine-toothed comb. First, about that victim selection process... the text is basically correct; the allocation constraints correctly reflect the total pages baseline: unconstrained covers all RAM and swap, cpuset uses the memory on the current cpuset nodes, mempolicy uses the current mempolicy's nodes, and memcg is constrained by the cgroup's limit. That seems right.

Now, concerning the OOM reaper... Ah, here's something that needs attention. The documentation states it calls `unmap_page_range()` to release physical pages. I'm pretty sure that function, as described, *doesn't* exist in the kernel anymore. The reaper uses `mas_for_each_rev` to walk the VMAs, and calls `zap_vma_for_reaping(vma)`, which then calls `__zap_vma_range()` to unmap the physical pages. That's a crucial distinction. It's not a *complete* falsehood, but referring to a non-existent function is not ideal and may cause confusion for anyone searching the kernel code. Time to file a docs patch for that.

Let me just double-check, but the documentation seems accurate when it says that the reaper walks the VMAs in reverse using `mas_for_each_rev` for reaping. The call to `zap_vma_for_reaping()` is the key.

Let's do a search just in case, but I am fairly certain that my statements are accurate after this inspection.

Okay, let's switch gears. I'm recalling the `process_mrelease` system call. The documentation explains that it lets userspace OOM managers trigger the same reaping mechanism on a dying process. Let's see... looking at the implementation of `process_mrelease`, it indeed calls `__oom_reap_task_mm(mm)` which *is* the exact same mechanism as the reaper. This is all good and correct.

Now, about `oom_score_adj`... Yes, setting it to -1000 *does* disable OOM killing for that process, which I can verify.

Finally, the `vm.panic_on_oom` setting. Is the description accurate for that? Okay, `0` kills a process, `1` panics only on unconstrained OOM, and `2` always panics. The statement that "1: panic on unconstrained OOM only" is accurate. And yes, my memory is correct. The `check_panic_on_oom` function does exactly what I expect. It panics only if `oc->constraint == CONSTRAINT_NONE`. The kernel *doesn't* panic for cpuset, mempolicy, or memcg allocation failures. Just to be absolutely sure, I'm going to quickly reference the `Documentation/admin-guide/sysctl/vm.rst` file. Yup, everything is lining up as I would expect. Okay, I'm satisfied.

      "problem": "The documentation incorrectly states that the OOM reaper calls `unmap_page_range()`.",
      "severity": "Low",
      "severity_explanation": "The documentation claims the OOM reaper calls `unmap_page_range()` to release physical pages. However, in `mm/oom_kill.c`, the OOM reaper uses `mas_for_each_rev()` and calls `zap_vma_for_reaping()` (which internally uses `__zap_vma_range()`). `unmap_page_range()` does not exist in this context. This is a low-severity issue as it only affects code discoverability and developer understanding, with no operational or runtime impact."
    },
    {
      "problem": "The documentation for `vm.oom_kill_allocating_task` fails to mention it is completely ignored during memory cgroup (memcg) OOM events.",
      "severity": "Medium",
      "severity_explanation": "The documentation states that setting `vm.oom_kill_allocating_task` to a non-zero value kills the task that triggered the OOM rather than scanning the task list. However, in `mm/oom_kill.c`'s `out_of_memory()`, the code explicitly requires `!is_memcg_oom(oc)` before evaluating this sysctl. This means the knob has absolutely no effect on memcg-triggered OOMs. This is a medium-severity issue because it has a visible real-life effect: system administrators configuring containerized environments may incorrectly rely on this sysctl and experience unexpected OOM killer behavior."
    },
    {
      "problem": "The documentation incorrectly omits swap space from the `Cpuset` and `Memory policy` `totalpages` baselines.",
      "severity": "Medium",
      "severity_explanation": "The documentation states that the `totalpages` baseline for Cpuset and Memory policy constraints is strictly 'memory on nodes...'. However, in `constrained_alloc()`, `oc->totalpages` is explicitly initialized with `total_swap_pages` before adding the node-specific RAM for both of these constraints. This is a medium-severity issue because it has a visible real-life effect: users relying on the documentation to manually calculate exact `oom_score_adj` values for constrained processes will produce incorrect results, potentially leading to critical processes being killed unintentionally."
    }
  ]
}

    "thought": "**My Analysis of OOM Documentation Inaccuracies**

Okay, so I've been digging into some documentation discrepancies related to the Linux kernel's Out-Of-Memory (OOM) handling, and I've come up with a few key findings. Let's break these down, and I'll lay out my thoughts on the severity of each.

First, I see the documentation incorrectly describes the OOM reaper as calling `unmap_page_range()`.  From my understanding of the current code, the modern function is actually either `zap_vma_for_reaping()` or, internally, `__zap_vma_range()`. While this API documentation is incorrect, it seems like a relatively low-impact issue. It could confuse developers reading the docs, but it doesn't appear to affect runtime behavior. I'm rating that one as Low severity.

Second, I noted that the documentation for `vm.oom_kill_allocating_task` doesn't mention that it's ignored when a memory cgroup (memcg) triggers an OOM situation. In fact, `!is_memcg_oom(oc)` is checked before the sysctl is applied.  This omission is a bigger deal because it affects how sysadmins will configure their systems. A user might set this, expecting it to apply to their containers, and be surprised when it doesn't work as they expect. This definitely has a visible real-life effect, so I'm calling this a Medium severity issue.

Finally, and this is where it gets interesting, the documentation doesn't explicitly state that swap space is included in the `totalpages` baselines used for `Cpuset` and `Memory policy` constraints. However, I see in `constrained_alloc()` that `oc->totalpages` is actually initialized with `total_swap_pages` in both cases. Now, this documentation omission is Medium because it has a real-world effect. Since the OOM score adjustment calculation relies on `totalpages`, incorrect documentation will produce incorrect calculation results. Since someone is likely using the documentation to set `oom_score_adj` for a process in a cpuset, their calculation will be completely wrong if swap is heavily used, potentially leading to unintended processes being killed. This could lead to a tangible, visible effect.

Just to be thorough, I went back and re-checked whether `total_swap_pages` is included in `CONSTRAINT_MEMCG`. In `constrained_alloc()`, with `is_memcg_oom(oc)`, it's `mem_cgroup_get_max()`, which is usually the memory limit (in memcg v2), and matches the documentation's claim of "cgroup's memory limit".

Also, I verified that `!is_memcg_oom(oc)` explicitly bypasses the `vm.oom_kill_allocating_task` check, so the code matches what I thought.

And I confirmed that `unmap_page_range` doesn't exist in mm/ and is only used in KMSAN and IOREMAP.

So, taking another look at these against the "False Positive Prevention Guide", none of these are related to defensive programming requests, API misuse assumptions, unverifiable assumptions, or performance tradeoffs, so I feel pretty confident in my ratings.

Docs/mm: document the OOM killer

This commit documents the OOM killer in mm/oom.rst. It covers the scoring
heuristic, allocation constraints, OOM reaper, process_mrelease syscall,
and sysctl knobs.

> +

> +- **Cpuset**: memory on nodes in the current cpuset.
> +- **Memory policy**: memory on nodes in the current mempolicy.

Should swap space also be included for the Cpuset and Memory policy
constraints?

Looking at constrained_alloc() in mm/oom_kill.c, oc->totalpages is
initialized with total_swap_pages before adding the node-specific RAM for
both of these constraints.

> +- **Memory cgroup**: the cgroup's memory limit.

[ ... ]

> +The reaper gives the victim a short window to exit naturally before
> +intervening.  It walks the victim's VMAs in reverse and calls
> +``unmap_page_range()`` to release physical pages.  Once reaping completes

This isn't a bug, but does the OOM reaper actually call unmap_page_range()?

In mm/oom_kill.c, the OOM reaper uses mas_for_each_rev() and calls
zap_vma_for_reaping(), which internally uses __zap_vma_range(). The
unmap_page_range() function does not exist in this context.

> +(or is no longer possible), the mm is marked ``MMF_OOM_SKIP`` so the OOM

[ ... ]

> +``vm.oom_kill_allocating_task``
> +  When non-zero, kill the task that triggered the OOM rather than scanning
> +  for the largest process.

Does this sysctl apply to memory cgroup (memcg) OOM events?

In out_of_memory() within mm/oom_kill.c, the code explicitly requires
!is_memcg_oom(oc) before evaluating this sysctl, meaning it is ignored
during memcg OOMs.

> +
> +``vm.oom_dump_tasks``
